author: @daveyrogers7336
text: I know how they could save this show. We the viewers start realizing that this is not really "Star Fleet Academy", but "Star Fleet Correctional Institution for the Criminally Insane" and that the students and the crew have all been tricked into believing that it's an Academy. They realize little by little that it's all a giant holodeck to keep them from causing trouble in the galaxy.  They are the worst and most dangerous people in the Federation, not the best. Second season is them trying to escape and we, the audience rooting against them. Hey, at least it's something.

replies.author: @sillypuppy5940
replies.text: Yes. Commander Jones is tall in episode one. At the end of the season, he is still tall. That's a non-arc, a non-story. Couldn't he bump his head on a low ceiling and come to realize the problems of being tall? Of course not; that is implied criticism of his tallness, and any hint of "tallism" is forbidden. All the writers can do is have him complain about how chairs and tables are too small for him - and that's some sort of terrible institutional tallism. Meanwhile he gets involved with all the adventures of his non-tall colleagues. So actually his height is of no relevance when the Klingons are on the starboard bow.

author: @CPDystopia
text:
Well to be fair, they did do character development. They simply forgot to remember in later episodes, and in a couple episodes...the same episode, that all those characters had development at all. 

Tarima, princess of Betazed is naive about worldly things but then is self absorbed blaming all her problems on everyone else and assumes to know how all her peers are now afraid of her after she saved everyone's lives through space magic, and then she is abundently aware of the attempts to psychoanalyse the students through theatre, but goes back to being princess betazed free of emotional and mental trauma by the next episode. 

Darem, overachiever desperately wants validation from his family who doesn't bother to write to him a single time or attend the wedding ceremony to become royalty of his entire species. He also has his life chosen for him since he was a child and finally realizes there's more to life through the academy. But when he opens up he is punished for witholding that information from the cadets, and is forced to abdicate his throne from his "best friend" who blames him for having personal growth from life experiences so she can congratulate herself for being the 1st queen in the history of her people in 1000's of years. Amazing how backwards their people are to have never developed social equality but developed transportation portals that negate the need for warp drive, which the rest of the universe doesn't bother to learn how to develop after you know all the dilithium blew up in the galaxy. 

Jay-Den - is a vegetarian, and a bird watcher, but also drinks blood wine and eats some warrior soup after deceiving the Klingon empire into some mock battle with starfleet so they can claim a planet starfleet wasn't using anyways through conquest. And he's gay, which is repeated constantly because the writers think that gender politics is an engine of narration some how. Also Klingons are now a marginallized people whose family structures have evolved into bisexual polycules? in 800 years. Well social change that rapid must mean there was faction of queer Klingons that genocided their way acroos the empire to enforce such a drastic change on their people, while also maintaining the warrior tradition of their people. Interesting. 

Caleb - can do everything magically, super smart was a hardened walled off criminal that survived alone from the tender age of 8 years old having escaped a detention center on a barren inhospitable world. 18 ish years go by of being alone having to fend for himself doing who the hell knows what is immediately reverted to a child like state, afraid and unsure about his feelings while he's in his cadet's uniform. He's the big macho glistening muscles to induce those snail trails but he is also emotionally broken and needs to be nurtured cared for and disciplined regularly. oooh la la. gross. 

SAM - retarded. rewritten to be super OP but ignores all of her features so that the plot can be told the writer's way even though SAM can fix everything in the blink of an eye. 

Nahle - no growth. she just stumbles awkwardly through one situation after another ignoring protocol and her contemporaries feelings and boundaries while exposing her students to one life threatening situation after another. 

There's more but it's the same thing they grow. and then that growth is forgotten about almost immediately. This is a terrible show. The only thing I'm focussing on is how many plot holes also have plot holes which is hard to do!

"The Operative", from 'Serenity', had MORE Character Development in the video-call with Mal after the attack on 'Haven' than all of those "cadets" combined!!!
But, then again, Joss Whedon could actually write!!!

author: @pwgdeathhawk8146
text: watch season 1 of Enterprise you know the characters names and their motivations by the end of episode 1, and by the end of season 4 T'pol has gone from humanities biggest hater to its biggest advocate, what an arc.

author: @rusharnmal7425
text: It was the greatest sin of episode 8 is that Holly Hunter's character is an extremely long lived being, and the Doctor is immortal being with perfect memory, the interaction between these characters should have been a reflection on grief and loss, and that they are determining the future for another immortal who is just starting with perfect memory, and what the implications are for how the teach and interact with this new young immortal.

replies.author: @gwenjackson8583
replies.text: That would have been actually interesting…to explore the implications of immortality.  But I guess that’s too deep for this show.  They’d rather explore Jay Den’s (oh my God) inexplicable attractiveness to all the male characters.  Why do they all love him?  He’s so unpleasant and annoying.
